summary: This post compares San Juan and Race for the Galaxy, two card games that share a common origin as card game adaptations inspired by Puerto Rico. Both games use hand management and tableau building mechanics where players build cards by discarding others, gaining special powers and victory points. The key difference is that Race is deeper and more complex, using an icon system that some find efficient once learned but others find off-putting. Fowler concludes that both games have merit: Race offers greater depth while San Juan is more accessible for teaching new players.
key_points: Both games descended from Puerto Rico, with designers Tom Lehmann (Race) and Andreas Seyfarth (San Juan/Puerto Rico) initially working together before diverging
The role selection mechanism creates player interaction - predicting opponents' choices and weighing whether your selection helps them more than you
Race uses icons to describe card abilities while San Juan uses text, creating a significant accessibility difference between the games
The production and trading engine provides both strategic depth and player interaction through timing optimization
Despite Race's greater complexity, both games play quickly and work well as filler games
money_quotes: The essence of their differences is that Race is the deeper and more complex game, so appeals more to people who are comfortable with more complexity in rules and gameplay.
If I can predict what my opponents will pick for roles, I may be able to use their role choice, allowing me to pick another role that will help me.
It's hard to know if this will bother you unless you play a couple of games to try it out.
For me it's the good kind of randomness: good players will mostly beat poor play, but less skillful players still have a chance to steal the occasional win.
I've since come to value San Juan as a complement - it's easier to teach to new people and works for those who can't grasp the icons.
key_insight: Two games sharing common ancestry can coexist in a collection when they serve different accessibility needs despite similar core mechanics.
